coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of defect-rich nitrogen and sulfur Co-doped graphene quantum dots as metal-free electrocatalyst for the oxygen reduction reaction
coredata.dc:creator: Fan, Tianju
Zhang, Guangxing
Jian, Lingfeng
Murtaza, Imran
Meng, Hong
Liu, Yidong
Min, Yong
coredata.dc:description: Carbon-based metal-free nanomaterials have attracted immense and persistent attention as electrocatalysts for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) in fuel cells, due to their high electrocatalytic activity, long-term stability and low cost. Here, a facile hydrothermal treatment in the presence of ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH) and sodium sulfide (Na2S), as precursors, has been developed to synthesize heteroatom-doped graphene quantum dots (GQDs). For comparison, undoped graphene quantum dots (GQDs), nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs), sulfur doped graphene quantum dots (S-GQDs) and nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots (N,S-GQDs) have been synthesized with the same method. The as-obtained N,S-GQDs possess a high N-doping content up to 9.36% and low S-doping content up to 0.78%, with a uniform size distribution similar to that of GQDs, N-GQDs and S-GQDs. Interestingly, the multi-types of NC or CSC bonding in metal free N,S-GQDs can significantly enhance the electrocatalytic activity for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), with the electron transfer number as good as 3.82, which verify the importance of our facile synthesis method to fabricate metal-free electrocatalysts for ORR and other electrochemical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen and sulfur Co-doped graphene quantum dots
Metal-free electrocatalyst
Oxygen reduction reaction

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Science and Technology Planning Project of Shenzhen Municipality
Shenzhen Science and Technology Innovation Commission
Guangdong Innovative and Entrepreneurial Research Team Program
National Basic Research Program of China (973 Program)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the formation of N,S-GQDs by one-pot method.

a) TEM image of N,S-GQDs/rGO, and b) HRTEM image of N,S-GQDs, the lattice spacing is 0.24 nm for N,S-GQDs (inset), c) AFM image of N,S-GQDs, d) the thickness of N,S-GQDs, and e) the size fraction of N,S-GQDs.
Raman spectra of GQDs/rGO and N,S-GQDs/rGO.
a) XPS survey spectrum of N,S- GQDs. High-resolution N1s and S2p spectra (insets).

The optical properties of N,S-GQDs. (a) UV/Vis absorption spectra of the N,S-GQDs, (b) The surface state determines the PL of N,S-GQDs. (c) The PL spectra of GQDs. (d) The PL spectra of N,S-GQDs in aqueous solutions.

Electrochemical ORR catalytic performance of N,S-codoped GQDs supported on rGO. a) CV curves of N,S-GQDs supported on rGO in N2 and O2 saturated 0.1 M KOH at a sweep rate of 10 mVs−1. b) LSV Contrast data of rGO, GQDs/rGO, N-GQDs/rGO, S-GQDs/rGO, N,S-GQDs/rGO and Pt-C at 1600 rotation rates. c) LSV of N,S-GQDs/rGO at different rotation rates. d) Koutecky-Levich plots at different potentials for N,S-GQDs/rGOs.
Electron transfer number (n) of Pt-C and GQDs, N-GQDs, S-GQDs and N,S-GQDs supported on rGO.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of defect-rich nitrogen and sulfur Co-doped graphene quantum dots as metal-free electrocatalyst for the oxygen reduction reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Advanced Materials, Peking University Shenzhen Graduate School, Shenzhen 518055, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials and Energy, Guangdong University of Technology, Guangzhou 510006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, International Islamic University, Islamabad 44000, Pakistan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon-based metal-free nanomaterials have attracted immense and persistent attention as electrocatalysts for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) in fuel cells, due to their high electrocatalytic activity, long-term stability and low cost. Here, a facile hydrothermal treatment in the presence of ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH) and sodium sulfide (Na2S), as precursors, has been developed to synthesize heteroatom-doped graphene quantum dots (GQDs). For comparison, undoped graphene quantum dots (GQDs), nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs), sulfur doped graphene quantum dots (S-GQDs) and nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots (N,S-GQDs) have been synthesized with the same method. The as-obtained N,S-GQDs possess a high N-doping content up to 9.36% and low S-doping content up to 0.78%, with a uniform size distribution similar to that of GQDs, N-GQDs and S-GQDs. Interestingly, the multi-types of NC or CSC bonding in metal free N,S-GQDs can significantly enhance the electrocatalytic activity for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), with the electron transfer number as good as 3.82, which verify the importance of our facile synthesis method to fabricate metal-free electrocatalysts for ORR and other electrochemical applications.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Nitrogen and sulfur Co-doped graphene quantum dots

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) is one of the most crucial factors that dominates in energy storage and conversion devices, for instance, metal-air batteries and fuel cells, as well as other applications including water purification, oxygen detection and corrosion protection [1–3]. To our knowledge, during the past few decades, researchers have focused on the platinum (Pt)-based or palladium (Pd)-based electrocatalysts, which have excellent electrocatalytic activity. However, their commercialization has been impeded by the high cost, limited resources, poor stability and deactivation by CO [4,5]. Therefore, exploration of superior performance Pt-free or metal-free electrocatalysts has intrigued scientists interests from both industrial and academic perspectives [6,7]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to search for new and inexpensive metal-free catalysts for ORRs.

Metal-free carbon-based electrocatalysts for ORR are relatively low cost, show excellent catalytic performance and CO tolerance, as compared to Pt based catalysts. In particular, heteroatom-doped nanocarbon materials, including carbon nanotubes, graphene and porous carbon, exhibit good stability and electrocatalytic performance for the ORR [8,9].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a new type of quasi zero-dimensional graphene nanostructures of size below 10 nm, with all the features of graphene in addition to their edge effects and quantum confinement [10]. Especially, GQDs feature underlying affluent edge sites that contribute to electrocatalytic activity and photodegradation activity [11,12]. Therefore, development of efficient methods for the preparation of GQDs has gained researchers' interest [13–16]. Jin et al. [17] investigated the ORR and found that GQDs cleave the graphene nanoribbons and result in superb electrocatalytic activity in alkaline media. Yang et al. [18] reported that graphene doped with sulfur and selenium also show better catalytic performance than the commercial Pt/C in alkaline media.

Furthermore, it has already been proved that co-doping carbon with two heteroatoms, S and N, can effectively create more catalytically active sites than singularly doped counterparts, which is attributed to the synergistic coupling effects between heteroatoms. The reduced graphene oxide (rGO) can be super doped with N via fluorination by ammonia, where N-doping level of N-superdoped rGO (NG) can reach up to 15.58 at%, which can be useful for energy storage applications [19–21]. However, high content N doped GQDs, in addition to the abundant features on the edge have been rarely reported.

Herein, we report facile synthesis of N, S co-doped GQDs (N,S-GQDs). The results reveal that the synthesized N,S-GQDs are endowed with unique structural features. Benefiting from a lot of defects caused by small size and synergistic effects of N and S co-doping, N,S-GQDs are promising candidates for metal-free electrocatalysts. The introduction of S to N-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) changes the state of N species, creates asymmetrical spin and charge density and causes the synergistic effect of N and S, which facilitates the electron transfer in ORR. Therefore, the resulting N,S-GQDs have potential applications as metal-free catalysts for ORR.

Graphite powder (400 mesh) was purchased from Xian-Feng Nano Co., Ltd. Whereas, potassium permanganate, concentrated sulfuric acid, sodium hydroxide, concentrated hydrochloric acid, hydrogen peroxide (30%), ammonium hydroxide (25%), sodium sulfide, hydroiodic (55%) and all reagents were analytical grade, purchased from Beijing Chemical Reagents.

Graphite and Na2S were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All chemicals were analytical grade and were used without further purification. GO was synthesized from natural graphite (2000 mesh) by modified Hummers' method as previously reported [22].

In a typical preparation procedure, a mixture of GO aqueous solution (20 mL, 3.0 mg/mL), ammonia solution (0.6 mL, 28 wt%), and Na2S was stirred to form a homogeneous solution and then transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave. The sealed autoclave was heated in an electric oven at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling down the autoclave to room temperature, the obtained solution was filtered using a 0.02 mm Millipore membrane. The obtained supernatant was then collected and dialyzed against deionized water to eliminate excess ammonia, sulfate radical, and carbonate ions. The resultant N,S-GQDs were obtained with consistent sizes of 3–8 nm.

3.5 mL aqueous solution of the synthesized N,S-GQDs (1 mg/mL) was added to 3.5 mL GO aqueous solution (2 mg/mL). After 0.5 h of ultrasonication, the solution was transferred to a 10 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 6 h. N,S-GQDs and rGO were compounded after the hydrothermal treatment. Finally, the N,S-GQDs/rGO solution was obtained and vacuum dried for use in the electrochemical measurements.

A mixture of nanosized GO aqueous solution (20 mL, 3.0 mg/mL) was stirred to form a homogeneous solution, transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and the same typical hydrothermal treatment, as mentioned above, was adopted to synthesize GQDs.

Finally, 3.5 mL aqueous solution of the synthesized GQDs (1 mg/mL) was added to 3.5 mL GO aqueous solution (2 mg/mL), to obtain the GQDs/rGO solution, following the same procedure, as mentioned in the last section for N,S-GQDs/rGO. The obtained solution was then vacuum dried for use in the electrochemical measurements.

The morphologies of the samples were investigated via field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, ZEISS Supra 55) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, 200 kV). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) analyses were performed by using a spectrometer (ESCALAB 250XL). Electrochemical experiments were conducted by using a computer-controlled potentiometer (CHI 760D, CH Instrument, USA). A conventional three-electrode cell was employed incorporating a carbon glass rotating ring-disk (RRDE, Pine Research Instrumentation) as working electrode, an Ag/AgCl, KCl (3 M) electrode as reference electrode, and a Pt electrode as counter electrode. All potentials were measured and reported vs. the Ag/AgCl, KCl (3 M) reference electrode. For the ORR, the experiments were carried out in O2 saturated 0.1 M KOH solution. The potential was cyclically scanned between −1.2 and + 0.2 V at a scan rate of 100 mV/s at the ambient temperature after purging O2 or Ar gas for 15 min. RRDE measurements were conducted at different rotation rates from 225 to 2500 rpm.

The general methods for electrochemical measurement, CV and LSV measurements, were performed with a typical three-electrode cell. A rotating disk electrode (RDE) made of glassy carbon was used as the working electrode, while a platinum wire and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) were used as the counter and reference electrodes, respectively. All experiments were conducted at room temperature, in O2 or N2-saturated 0.1 M of KOH solution (KOH, certified ACS grade). The scanning rates for the CV and LSV measurements were 50 and 10 mV/s, respectively, under various rotating speeds.

The working electrodes were prepared as follows: 2 mg each of GQDs/rGO, N-GQDs/rGO, S-GQDs/rGO and N,S-GQDs/rGO were separately added in 0.1 mL of Nafion solution (5 wt%) and 1.9 mL of ethanol before sonication for 30 min. Then 10 μL of each of the resulting suspensions was dropped onto pre-polished glassy carbon RDE (D = 5 mm) and dried at room temperature to obtain working electrodes based on each type of GQDs. As a comparison the commercial Pt-C electrocatalyst (with a Pt-C loading of 20 wt%) suspension was also prepared by dispersing 10 mg of Pt-C powder in ethanol (2 mL) in the presence of 50 μL of Nafion in ethanol (5 wt%).

Fig. 1 illustrates the fabrication process of N,S-codoped GQDs. Pan et al. [23] have reported a simple hydrothermal approach for the cutting of graphene sheets into blue-luminescent GQDs. Zhang et al. [24] have reported the synthesis of N,S-GQDs via one-step hydrothermal method by using ammonia, powdered sulfur and oxidized graphene. However, in this study, nanosized GO is synthesized from 2000 mesh natural graphite by Hummers' method, followed by the preparation of N,S-GQDs, N-GQDs and S-GQDs using a facile and environment friendly hydrothermal route, as illustrated in Fig.S1, S2 and S3, respectively.

Fig. 2 a shows typical transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the synthesized N,S-GQDs/rGO. It is found that their diameter ranges from 5 to 10 μm. The High Resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of the synthesized N,S-GQDs, with diameter ranging from 3 to 6 nm, is shown in Fig. 2b. Moreover, the lattice structure of the N,S-GQDs can be clearly found in the HRTEM image shown in Fig. 2b (inset) with the lattice spacing distance of 0.24 nm, corresponding to the graphene lattice [25], indicating the high crystallinity of the synthesized N,S-GQDs. The atomic force microscope (AFM) image of the N,S-GQDs in Fig. 2c shows that the heights of N,S-GQDs are mainly distributed in the range of 2–4 nm with average height of 2.4 nm as shown in Fig. 2d, which is similar to that reported previously [26].

Meanwhile, we also obtained TEM images of GQDs, N-GQDs and S-GQDs, which show consistent morphology and size with that of N,S-GQDs, with the lattice spacing of 0.23–0.24 nm as shown in Fig. S4a, Fig. S5a and Fig. S6a, respectively. The detailed analyses of HRTEM images for GQDs, N-GQDs, S-GQDs and N,S-GQDs reveal that their periphery consists of mixed zigzag and armchair edges and the size distribution of GQDs, N-GQDs, S-GQDs and N,S-GQDs is uniform ranging from 2 to 8 nm as shown in Fig. S4b, Fig. S5b, Fig. S6b and Fig. 2e.

Raman spectra (Fig. 3 ) of N,S-GQDs/rGO and GQDs/rGO exhibit two remarkable peaks around 1594 and 1348 cm−1, respectively. The G band is attributed to the first-order scattering of the E2g mode of sp2 carbon atoms, the disorder makes the D band to increase, and the ID/IG ratio is used as an evaluation of GQDs edge quality such as the relative edge roughness/defect-density [27]. The D band around 1348 cm−1 is related to the defects in GQDs/rGO. Moreover, the intensity ratios (ID/IG) of N,S-GQDs/rGO and GQD/rGOs are 1.08 and 0.92, respectively, indicating higher edge roughness and rich defects in N,S-GQDs, and thus low crystallinity, than GQDs.

XPS spectrum of N,S-GQDs is shown in Fig. 4 , the elemental contents of C, N and S in N,S-GQDs are 81.86 at%, 9.36 at% and 0.78 at%, respectively. The low content of S reveals that it is difficult to dope N,S-GQDs with sulfur. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum (inset N) of N,S-GQDs supports the presence of both pyridine N (398.6 eV) and pyrrolic N (400.6 eV) species. Furthermore, the inset S, indicating the S2p spectra, suggests that there are two forms of sulfur in N,S-GQDs. One is attributed to sulfide groups (–C–S–C–,163.3 eV) and the other belongs to the state of oxidized sulfur groups (–C–SOx–C–, 168.5 eV). The results show that N and S elements have been successfully doped in GQDs which is very useful as N and S co-doped in carbon materials have been known as one of the most probably active sites in ORR [28]. Consequently, the electrochemical performance is potentially enhanced due to the synergistic effect of N and S in N,S-GQDs.

The optical properties of monodispersed GQDs and N,S-GQDs have been investigated by UV–vis absorption spectra as shown in Fig. 5 a. A typical absorption peak at about 337 nm is observed, which is similar to that of the reported GQDs. The PL excited state of N,S-GQDs dispersion indicates the π–π*, n–π* and surface state transitions in Fig. 5b. As in the reported GQDs [29], The n–π* transition and the surface state belong to N,S-GQDs and the π–π* transition is assigned to the aromatic sp2 domains as well as the hybrid structure of the partial carbon skeleton and edge groups.

Fig. 5c and d shows the PL spectra of GQDs and N,S-GQDs, respectively, which exhibit wavelength dependent PL behavior. In the case of GQDs (Fig. 5c), the emission wavelength shifts gradually from 448 to 526 nm with the excitation wavelength from 340 to 430 nm, demonstrating a maximum excitation wavelength of 340 nm corresponding to the emission wavelength of 460 nm. However, in the case of N,S-GQDs (Fig. 5d), the emission wavelength shifts from 452 to 522 nm, with the excitation wavelength shift from 340 to 430 nm, depicting a maximum excitation wavelength of 340 nm corresponding to the emission wavelength of 452 nm. The size and surface states are responsible for this novel property, as Tang's group has indicated that the inter-system crossing and adjacent vibrational relaxation of excited electrons are responsible for this behavior [30,31]. Nevertheless, the PL intensity of N,S-GQDs is markedly enhanced compared with GQDs excited with 365 nm (Fig. 5c and d). Obviously, the N, S-dopant may be responsible for the difference. The defects in GQDs increase with the introduction of N and S dopant according to the Raman spectra. These rich defects are positively charged sites that are essential to the optical property [32].

It is difficult to directly apply pristine GQDs, N-GQDs, S-GQDs and N,S-GQDs for electrochemical applications, due to poor electrical conductivity. In order to overcome this drawback, pristine GQDs, N-GQDs, S-GQDs and N,S-GQDs were anchored on conductive graphene. As we know that graphene is a more promising substrate because of its excellent conductivity and electrochemical properties [33], and pristine and doped GQDs can be uniformly distributed on graphene sheets.

The electrocatalytic activity of GQDs, N-GQDs, S-GQDs and N,S-GQDs supported on rGO sheets is investigated by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) by using a conventional three-electrode system. The ORR catalytic performance of N,S-GQDs/rGO and S-GQDs/rGO is measured by cyclic voltammetry (CV) from −0.9 V to +0.1 V (Fig. 6 a, Fig. S8a), which displays capacitive currents in O2- saturated and N2- saturated electrolyte. It can be seen that the oxygen reduction peaks are observed for all the GQD composites. The reduction peak of the N,S-GQDs/rGO composite is found at −0.38 V, and the most positive reduction peak of 0.26 V and high peak current density of −0.96 mAcm−2 is measured for the N,S-GQDs/rGO composite. These results suggest that the N,S-GQDs/rGO composite exhibits a faster oxygen reduction reaction to enhance electrocatalytic activity as compared to that of the undoped GQDs/rGO, N-GQDs/rGO and S-GQDs/rGO.

Furthermore, the electrocatalytic activity of N,S-GQDs/rGO, N-GQDs/rGO, S-GQDs/rGO and GQDs/rGO towards the ORR is investigated by Linear Sweep Voltammetry (LSV) using a rotating disk electrode (RDE). Fig. 6b shows the resulting LVS curves of N,S-GQDs/rGO, N-GQDs/rGO, S-GQDs/rGO, GQDs/rGO and commercial Pt/C at a rotation speed of 1600 rpm in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH electrolyte. Being an important parameter of the electrocatalytic activity for ORR, the onset potential of oxygen reduction is determined from the intersection of the tangents drawn on the baseline and the signal current. The onset potentials of the N,S-GQDs/rGO, GQDs/rGO, N-GQDs/rGO, and S-GQDs/rGO composites and rGO are −0.11, 0.22, −0.16, −0.18 and −0.21 V, respectively. When compared to the electrocatalytic activity of the commercial Pt/C catalyst, an ORR onset potential of −0.11 V and a limiting current density of 4.82 mA cm−2 for the N,S-GQDs/rGO composite are close to those of Pt/C (−0.08 V and 5.08 mA cm−2). Moreover, the half-wave potential of the N,S-GQDs/rGO composite is −0.28 V which is close to the commercial Pt/C (−0.29 V).

To gain further insight into the ORR process of N,S-GQDs/rGO, GQDs/rGO, S-GQDs/rGO and N-GQDs/rGO, linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) measurements are performed as shown in Fig. 6c, Fig. S7a, Fig. S8b and Fig. S9a, which are carried out on a RDE at various rotation speeds between 225 and 2025 rpm in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution. Due to the shortened diffusion layers at higher speeds, these data show the increase in current density with an increase in rotation speed.

As an important parameter for determining the kinetics of the ORR process, the Koutecky-Levich (K-L) plots of the N,S-GQDs/rGO at different potentials are shown in Fig. 6d, which suggest first order kinetics toward the ORR process. The electron transfer number (n) is calculated to be 3.82 for N,S-GQDs from the slope of the K-L plots, using the Levich equation. Fig. S7b and Fig. S9b show the corresponding K-L plots for GQDs and N-GQDs at different potentials, and the electron transfer numbers for GQDs and N-GQDs are 2.58 and 3.19, respectively. Fig. 7 exhibits the electron transfer numbers (n) of N,S-GQDs/rGO, GQDs/rGO, S-GQDs/rGO, N-GQDs/rGO and Pt/C as electrocatalysts. The electrocatalytic performance of the N,S-GQDs/rGO (n is 3.82) is much higher than that of the GQDs/rGO (n is 2.24), N-GQDs/rGO (n is 3.19), S-GQDs/rGO (n is 3.41) and comparable to that of the commercial Pt/C (n is 3.96). Moreover, the electron transfer numbers of different graphene-based materials used as metal free electrocatalysts and synthesized with different techniques are presented in Table S1. These results indicate that N,S-GQDs/rGO has high electron transfer number than reported carbon based doped materials.

It has been reported that the good linearity of all plots implies a first-order reaction with respect to the dissolved oxygen concentration and the number of electrons transferred per oxygen molecule involved in the ORR process, suggesting that both two-electron and four-electron procedures were included in the oxygen reduction [34,35]. To our knowledge, Chen et al. [36] have reported modification of pyridinic N and O-rich defects in a bifunctional electrocatalyst can enhance electrocatalytic performance. Hence, the good electrocatalytic performance is benefited from the doped N and S atoms having different electronegativities which are better than carbon [37,38]. However, the pyridinic-N and thiophene S have played an important role for enhancing electrocatalytic activity and attributed to the small size of GQDs. Therefore, the novel defect-rich N,S-GQDs supported on rGO can create more active sites for electrocatalytic reaction.

In summary, we have successfully synthesized N,S-GQDs via facile and environmentally friendly one-pot hydrothermal treatment of GO with NH3 and Na2S as N and S sources, respectively. The synthesized N,S-GQDs reveal high N-doping level up to 9.36%, and low S-doping level up to 0.78%, with enhanced electrocatalytic activity, compared to that of the GQDs, N-GQDs and S-GQDs, making it highly suitable for further employing as a metal-free catalyst for ORR. The introduction of S in N,S-GQDs changes the state of N species, creates asymmetrical spin and charge density and enables the synergetic effect of N and S, which facilitate the electron transfer in the ORR process. Therefore, the N,S-GQDs are predicted to have a promising future to substitute Pt-C based catalysts in fuel cells application.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support of Guangdong Innovative and Entrepreneurial Research Team Program (No. 2016ZT06C412), the International Cooperation and Exchange Program under grant between the National Natural Science Foundation of China and Pakistan Science Foundation (No. 5161101159 and PSF-NSFC-II/Eng/C-IIUI-06), the National Basic Research Program of China (973 Program, No. 2015CB856505), Shenzhen Science and Technology Research Grants (GJHZ20160229122304608), and Shenzhen Maker Special Project Program (GRCK20170424144331753).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jallcom.2019.04.097.
